result:
The first thing I notice when I wake up is the sound the house makes before anyone else is awake—not a single sound, really, but a braided rope of them: the refrigerator breathing like a large benign animal, the hot-water pipe ticking once as if a tiny metal heart inside the wall has cleared its throat, the weird hush that is actually the city traffic held in suspension by distance and wood-framed insulation. I lie still and try to decide whether I am the only person alive on the planet. The illusion never lasts more than four seconds, but I keep auditioning for it every morning anyway, the way some people keep buying lottery tickets. There is a small, selfish comfort in picturing the rest of creation temporarily paused, the way a VCR used to freeze on a single stripe-marbled frame when you pressed the still-advance button. I never wanted the world to end; I just wanted an intermission.

When I was eight I thought the future would arrive all at once, like a package taped shut with tomorrow’s date stamped on it. I pictured a corrugated cardboard box big enough to hold jetpacks, lunar condominiums, meals in pill form, and world peace. Instead the future has seeped in sideways, a slow leak rather than a delivery. One Tuesday you look at your telephone and realize it has more memory than the Apollo command module; another Thursday you catch yourself asking a cloud how traffic looks on the interstate; a random Wednesday you discover that your favorite childhood cartoon is now considered “period-accurate mid-century ephemera” in a graduate seminar. None of these arrive with a trumpet fanfare; they simply accumulate like silt until you notice your feet no longer touch the river bottom.

I live in a mid-sized city that insists on calling itself a town. City Hall runs a slogan contest every spring, and the winning entry gets painted in fresh white script on the brick wall of the old mill: A SMALL TOWN WITH A BIG HEART. The heartbeat in question is irregular. Some days it gallops—when the minor-league team wins in extra innings and fireworks pop over the river, when the university dormitories empty in May and the streets suddenly feel like lungs that have learned to inhale deeper. Other days the heartbeat slows so dramatically you can almost hear the blood pooling: vacant storefronts dressed up like brides with “For Lease” sashes, the hospital announcing another round of layoffs, teenagers overdosing in the Walmart parking lot because fentanyl is cheaper than beer. We are, depending on whom you ask, either a community in transition or a cautionary tale. The truth is we are both, the way a person can be both hungry and nauseous at the same time.

When I walk to the co-op for coffee I pass a mural someone started in 2020 and never finished. It was supposed to depict Breonna Taylor, but the artist left after the second coat of blue underpainting, so what remains is an outline of eyes floating above a wash that looks like twilight trapped under polyurethane. The wall has become a palimpsest of protests: stickers that say DEFUND, stickers that say REFUND THE POLICE, a sun-bleached QR code no one scans, a tiny green dinosaur stenciled at knee height for reasons no one can explain. Each layer is a fossil of whoever felt urgent on that particular afternoon. I like the mural precisely because it is unfinished. Completion would have turned it into a monument, and monuments get debated by city councils; instead it is still a conversation, even if most pedestrians pass it the way they pass wind—quickly, with practiced indifference.

Inside the co-op the barista knows my order but pretends to forget half the time, which is her way of keeping the ritual democratic. I admire the gesture even while I resent it. We all want to be known until we sense the knowledge might be used as leverage. She scribbles a foam heart on the surface of my cortado, a microscopic valentine I destroy with the first sip. While I wait for the milk to cool, I eavesdrop on two retirees discussing whether crows can recognize individual human faces. One insists they absolutely can, the other says that’s anthropomorphism run amok. They both look over at me as if I might adjudicate. I shrug, palms up, the universal sign for I contain multitudes and none of them are ornithologists. Outside, a crow lands on the electric wire directly in my line of sight, tilts its head, and caws twice. I choose to interpret this as solidarity rather than threat.

Back home I try to write, which is a pompous way of saying I open a blank document and watch the cursor blink like a metronome keeping time for no orchestra. The page is a country I keep attempting to immigrate to but whose customs form I can never fill out correctly. Some days I’m denied entry for insufficient sincerity; other days for excessive self-loathing. I have composed entire novels in the shower only to watch them evaporate by the time I locate a towel. I once read that the average person thinks sixty thousand thoughts per day, which works out to forty-one thoughts per minute while awake. I’m convinced at least half of mine are variations on Why can’t I remember the thing I promised myself I would not forget. The thing, of course, is different every day—buy dental floss, text my cousin happy birthday, apologize to the friend I ghosted in 2017, save the planet. The planet keeps spinning without my editorial oversight, a blue-green diva who refuses to learn her final cue.

I keep a notebook of strange compliments I’ve received. A woman on a bus told me I had “the kind of eyebrows that believe in justice.” A drunk poet at a wedding said my voice sounded like “a vinyl record being born.” A child once asked whether I was the human version of her night-light. Each compliment is a small mirror I glued to the inside of my skull; when the light hits them at the correct angle, the reflections rearrange into the silhouette of a person I almost recognize. I haul this mosaic self from room to room, hoping no one notices the grout is missing in places.

Loneliness is a shape-shifter. Sometimes it’s a cathedral hush that makes you lower your voice even when no one else is home. Sometimes it’s a low-grade fever that never spikes high enough to justify staying in bed. Occasionally it arrives as a sudden erotic charge directed at the wrong target—the FedEx driver’s forearm, the barista’s wrist flicking steamed milk, the person on the Zoom call who unmuted accidentally and sighed into the collective ether. These micro-crushes are less about the individuals than about the collision of their proximity and your hunger. You don’t want them; you want the version of you that lives inside their gaze for half a second, the version that is inevitably more coherent.

I have a theory that everyone is allotted a finite number of authentic screams per lifetime. Most people expend the majority during childhood—on ferris wheels, in dentist offices, at the sight of a sibling heading for their hidden diary. Adults hoard what remains, saving it for the proper occasion: a car crash, a cancer diagnosis, the moment they finally admit the marriage is over. The trouble is you never know when the tally has dropped to zero. You might open your mouth on the day the stock market crashes and discover nothing but a wheeze escapes, the same thin sound a balloon makes when you stretch its neck. I tested the theory once at the edge of the Grand Canyon at sunrise. I expected my shout to ricochet off the sandstone and return transformed, but what came back sounded like a grocery bag caught in a tree—rustle, surrender, silence. I counted that as one scream gone; I have no idea how many remain.

Memory is unreliable, but in ways so consistent you could set a clock by it. We forget the pain of childbirth, the taste of last Tuesday’s lunch, the exact wording of the insult that kept us awake for a week. We remember the dress we wore to eighth-grade graduation, the smell of the basement where we lost our virginity, the phone number of the house we lived in when we were five. The self is a museum curated by a committee of drunkards who keep mislabeling the exhibits. I can’t recall my maternal grandmother’s face unless I’m staring at the photograph in which she’s holding me on her lap, and yet I can perfectly reproduce the jingle of the ice-cream truck that prowled our neighborhood in 1987. Researchers call this survivorship bias of the trivial. I call it evidence that the universe has a sense of humor drier than month-old Christmas cookies.

The pandemic broke time into uneven chunks. March 2020 feels like a decade rendered in pencil, whereas the entire year 2022 feels like a single afternoon I misplaced. I remember stocking up on beans as if beans were the ark that would float us above the flood. I remember wiping down cereal boxes with bleach solution, my hands smelling like a public pool for weeks. I remember standing on the porch at 7 p.m. banging pots and pans together, an urban rain dance we performed for the benefit of nurses whose names we didn’t know. What I don’t remember is deciding to trust strangers again, and yet here we are, breathing the same canned air of the pharmacy, our masks tucked in pockets like lucky handkerchiefs we no longer believe in but carry just in case.

They say trauma lodges in the body, but nobody mentions that joy does too. The knot between my shoulder blades isn’t only twenty years of bad posture; it’s also the day I won the fifth-grade spelling bee and couldn’t stop shrugging. The tension in my jaw isn’t only nightly grinding; it’s also the first time I saw the ocean and stood speechless, mouth open like a broken mailbox. I carry both anvils and balloons in the same overburdened suitcase; sometimes I open it and can’t tell which is which because they’ve learned to imitate each other’s weight.

Last summer I tried to relearn how to swim. The instructor, a bronze-haired woman named Laurel, told me the problem was that I treated water like an audience I had to apologize to before every stroke. She said water didn’t need my confession; it had already forgiven me for whatever I thought I’d done. I laughed so hard I swallowed a mouthful of chlorinated regret. By the final lesson I managed to glide the length of the pool without hyperventilating. I emerged dripping and lighter by exactly one ancient guilt. Laurel high-fived me, then quietly added that the average human body contains enough atoms to fill twenty square miles if spread out at ground level. I pictured myself evaporating into a diffuse civic plaza, pedestrians walking through my protons unaware. The image felt like mercy wearing a party hat.

Love, contrary to popular propaganda, is not a drug; it is a weather system. It approaches from the west, announced by a darkening you can taste on your tongue hours before the clouds arrive. It dumps buckets of serotonin, then moves on, leaving you to mop the floors. Sometimes it lingers and forms a seasonal depression. Sometimes it spawns tornadoes that relocate your moral furniture to the next county. The mistake is assuming you are the storm; you are at best the barometer, at worst the trailer park. The only sane response is to buy sturdy boots and keep a battery-powered radio, but nobody ever takes that advice because the sky is so beautiful right before it splits open.

I have never been to space, but my cousin operates a drone that maps lunar landing sites for NASA. She sends me photos of craters so perfectly round they look like the cosmos tried its hand at pottery. She says the moon smells like spent gunpowder, a fact she knows indirectly because astronauts returned with the odor clinging to their suits. I asked whether the moon feels haunted; she replied that haunting requires memory, and the moon has never had an atmosphere to hold onto anything long enough to remember it. I think she was warning me about the dangers of bottling nostalgia where no wind can disperse it, but I may have been projecting.

The average person will speak 860,341,500 words in a lifetime, give or take a million monologues delivered to pets. I wonder how many of mine have already been wasted on small talk about humidity, on lies meant to protect people who no longer speak to me, on sentences that began as bridges and collapsed halfway across the ravine. I want a refund, or at least an itemized receipt so I can dispute the charges marked “regret.” The customer-service line is perpetually busy; the hold music is my own voice reciting every word I wish I could recall.

At night the city installs a second sky made of streetlights and LED billboards. From my window it looks like constellations assembled by committee, each star required to justify its wattage in triplicate. I mute the television and watch this artificial galaxy flicker. Sometimes a transformer blows and a quadrant goes dark; for a moment the ancient stars overhead regain their monopoly on wonder, and I remember that darkness is not empty but classified. Then the power returns, and the newer sky reasserts itself, brighter than before, as if embarrassed by its momentary absence. I draw the curtain and pretend to sleep, but mostly I catalog the ways I’ve failed to become the person the younger me imagined would rescue her. The inventory is lengthy, but the margins are roomy enough for revisions.

Occasionally I conduct thought experiments in parallel universes. There is one where I said yes to the job in Kyoto and now speak fluent Japanese with a Kansai accent. Another where I married the marine biologist and live on a boat named Maybe Tomorrow. A third where I never left my hometown, never experimented with sarcasm, never learned to distrust men who wear cowboy boots ironically. In that universe I teach high-school chemistry and coach the debate team, and every spring I judge the state tournament wearing the same tweed blazer I wore at seventeen. I am, by all accounts, content. The odd thing is that none of these parallel selves ever imagine me back. I am the only version who conducts cross-dimensional surveillance, which either makes me the most curious or the most voyeuristic, depending on your moral taxonomy.

Yesterday I found a crumpled Post-it note in a jacket I hadn’t worn since 2019. In my own handwriting it said: Tell her before the sun gets there. I have no memory of writing it, no idea who her refers to, no clue what the sun was racing toward. The ink has faded to the color of a bruise almost healed. I stood on the sidewalk and stared at the yellow square until a passerby asked if I was okay. I said yes, because that is the word my mouth dispenses automatically, like a vending machine calibrated for social expedience. The note is now taped to my bathroom mirror, a ransom demand from a past self who forgot to specify the currency. Each morning I brush my teeth and negotiate with the hostage.

The etymology of the word deadline began as a Civil War term for a line beyond which prisoners would be shot. Journalists later adopted it to describe the time beyond which their stories would be murdered. I think about this every time I confront the blank page and its silent ultimatum. I tell myself that no actual bullets are involved, but the adrenaline in my bloodstream disagrees. The body is a conspiracy theorist convinced that every ticking sound is a countdown. I keep writing less because I believe in the triumph of art and more because it’s the closest I can get to disarming the trap without stepping outside it.

When my mother turned sixty she asked me to shave her head. Chemotherapy had claimed clumps, and she wanted to preempt the mutiny. I stood in the backyard while dragonflies traced invisible alphabets above us. She wore a faded David Bowie T-shirt and held a margarita in a plastic cup shaped like a flamingo. The clippers buzzed like an angry bee hive. With each pass she quoted a line from a poem she’d taught me as a child: “I am not here to surrender.” When we finished, she looked like a marble bust of herself, serene and slightly amused. She handed me the margarita and said, “Now you can see every thought I’ve ever had.” I laughed until I realized she wasn’t joking. We sat on the porch and watched the sun retire. She died nine months later, but for the rest of that summer I carried the image of her skull glowing like a lighthouse that had decided to guide ships through herself rather than across any sea.

Grief is a shapewear garment you forget you’re wearing until you try to take a deep breath. It reshapes you whether or not the outfit matches your shoes. People expect you to “move on,” a phrase that implies grief is a stalled car blocking traffic. Instead it rides shotgun, commentary optional. Sometimes it turns the radio to a station that plays only breakup songs from the 1980s. Sometimes it rolls down the window and shouts questions at pedestrians: Do you miss anyone right now? Did you tell them? The only remedy I’ve found is to let it choose the music occasionally; every so often it picks a song that makes you laugh at the memory of singing off-key with the person who is gone, and for three minutes the garment loosens.

I read that the human skeleton regenerates itself every ten years, which means I am already three iterations removed from the girl who once carved her initials into a birch tree with a butter knife. The tree is still there, taller, its bark stretched so the letters look like an eye chart for ghosts. I visit it sometimes and press my palm against the trunk, pretending I can feel the pulse of everything I used to believe. The tree doesn’t remember me; growth rings have no nostalgia clause. Still, I apologize for vandalism and promise to do better. The leaves rustle, translating my guilt into oxygen, a transaction so elegant it almost feels like absolution.

The trouble with happiness is that it arrives disguised as ordinary hours. You expect trumpets, but you get Tuesday at 2 p.m., the smell of cinnamon, a text that says I saw this meme and thought of you. Only later, when the hour has hardened into memory, do you realize it was happiness, by which time it’s too late to thank it properly. You were busy waiting for the confetti that never came. I try now to salute the mundane as it passes, like a pedestrian nodding at strangers who may never speak but whose coexistence forms the invisible mortar of civic life. Most ignore the nod; some nod back; occasionally one person startles as if touched by static electricity. Those are the moments I pocket like smooth stones, reminders that we are all covert operatives in each other’s joy.

The future will come, not as a fleet of chrome-hovercars but as another Tuesday that refuses to introduce itself. I will open the curtains and the city will have rearranged its freckles overnight: a new coffee shop where the pawn shop used to be, a mural of a blooming heart where someone once spray-painted a slur, a tree uprooted by last night’s storm lying across the sidewalk like a sentence whose subject has vanished. I will step over it and keep walking, because that is the only plot available: continue, revise, rinse, repeat. My bones will quietly trade places; my neurons will file new amnesias; the sixty-thousand thoughts will queue again, impatient for their turn on the microphone. And somewhere in that stream will be a single syllable that remembers every version of me—the valedictorian, the vandal, the voyeur of parallel worlds, the swimmer still apologizing to water. That syllable will travel the length of my body looking for an exit, and when it finally reaches my mouth I will open it, unsure whether I am starting a sentence or ending one, trusting that whichever it is, the air—patient, unjudging—will carry it forward like every other atom it has agreed to host.

I tell myself that no actual bullets are involved, but the adrenaline in my bloodstream disagrees. The body is a conspiracy theorist convinced that every ticking sound is a countdown. I keep writing less because I believe in the triumph of art and more because it’s the closest I can get to disarming the trap without stepping outside it.

When my mother turned sixty she asked me to shave her head. Chemotherapy had claimed clumps, and she wanted to preempt the mutiny. I stood in the backyard while dragonflies traced invisible alphabets above us. She wore a faded David Bowie T-shirt and held a margarita in a plastic cup shaped like a flamingo. The clippers buzzed like an angry bee hive. With each pass she quoted a line from a poem she’d taught me as a child: “I am not here to surrender.” When we finished, she looked like a marble bust of herself, serene and slightly amused. She handed me the margarita and said, “Now you can see every thought I’ve ever had.” I laughed until I realized she wasn’t joking. We sat on the porch and watched the sun retire. She died nine months later, but for the rest of that summer I carried the image of her skull glowing like a lighthouse that had decided to guide ships through herself rather than across any sea.

Grief is a shapewear garment you forget you’re wearing until you try to take a deep breath. It reshapes you whether or not the outfit matches your shoes. People expect you to “move on,” a phrase that implies grief is a stalled car blocking traffic. Instead it rides shotgun, commentary optional. Sometimes it turns the radio to a station that plays only breakup songs from the 1980s. Sometimes it rolls down the window and shouts questions at pedestrians: Do you miss anyone right now? Did you tell them? The only remedy I’ve found is to let it choose the music occasionally; every so often it picks a song that makes you laugh at the memory of singing off-key with the person who is gone, and for three minutes the garment loosens.

I read that the human skeleton regenerates itself every ten years, which means I am already three iterations removed from the girl who once carved her initials into a birch tree with a butter knife. The tree is still there, taller, its bark stretched so the letters look like an eye chart for ghosts. I visit it sometimes and press my palm against the trunk, pretending I can feel the pulse of everything I used to believe. The tree doesn’t remember me; growth rings have no nostalgia clause. Still, I apologize for vandalism and promise to do better. The leaves rustle, translating my guilt into oxygen, a transaction so elegant it almost feels like absolution.

The trouble with happiness is that it arrives disguised as ordinary hours. You expect trumpets, but you get Tuesday at 2 p.m., the smell of cinnamon, a text that says I saw this meme and thought of you. Only later, when the hour has hardened into memory, do you realize it was happiness, by which time it’s too late to thank it properly. You were busy waiting for the confetti that never came. I try now to salute the mundane as it passes, like a pedestrian nodding at strangers who may never speak but whose coexistence forms the invisible mortar of civic life. Most ignore the nod; some nod back; occasionally one person startles as if touched by static electricity. Those are the moments I pocket like smooth stones, reminders that we are all covert operatives in each other’s joy.

The future will come, not as a fleet of chrome-hovercars but as another Tuesday that refuses to introduce itself. I will open the curtains and the city will have rearranged its freckles overnight: a new coffee shop where the pawn shop used to be, a mural of a blooming heart where someone once spray-painted a slur, a tree uprooted by last night’s storm lying across the sidewalk like a sentence whose subject has vanished. I will step over it and keep walking, because that is the only plot available: continue, revise, rinse, repeat. My bones will quietly trade places; my neurons will file new amnesias; the sixty-thousand thoughts will queue again, impatient for their turn on the microphone. And somewhere in that stream will be a single syllable that remembers every version of me—the valedictorian, the vandal, the voyeur of parallel worlds, the swimmer still apologizing to water. That syllable will travel the length of my body looking for an exit, and when it finally reaches my mouth I will open it, unsure whether I am starting a sentence or ending one, trusting that whichever it is, the air—patient, unjudging—will carry it forward like every other atom it has agreed to host.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
